title: Worked Example: Two-File ASDF System in the Sandbox

sections.markdown:
# Worked Example: Two-File ASDF System in the Sandbox

Let's stand up a real ASDF system from inside the sandbox. The sandbox supports per-session files via

```
PUT    /v1/sessions/{id}/files/{path}
GET    /v1/sessions/{id}/files
GET    /v1/sessions/{id}/files/{path}
DELETE /v1/sessions/{id}/files/{path}
```

Files you `PUT` land in `/var/sandbox/<session>/`, which is also your current directory when code runs. So you can `PUT` `cool.asd` and `cool.lisp` from outside, then load them inside.

**Step 1 — write `cool.asd`** (via the file API):

```lisp
(asdf:defsystem #:cool
  :description "A tiny example."
  :serial t
  :components ((:file "cool")))
```

**Step 2 — write `cool.lisp`** (via the file API):

```lisp
(defpackage #:cool
  (:use #:cl)
  (:export #:greet))

(in-package #:cool)

(defun greet (name)
  (format nil "hello, ~a" name))
```

**Step 3 — load it from the REPL inside the session:**

```lisp
(asdf:load-asd (merge-pathnames "cool.asd" (uiop:getcwd)))
(asdf:load-system :cool)
(cool:greet "world")   ; => "hello, world"
```

`load-asd` registers the system without needing it on the search path. `load-system` then loads the components.

The rest of this lesson exercises just the **load-asd-from-string** trick: rather than write to disk, we'll define the same system inline (via `defsystem`) and confirm we can call its functions. The file-API version is what you'd do in a real workflow; the inline version is what we can test deterministically here.

sections.explain: Inline simulation of the worked example: define the system, define the package, define the function (qualified so we don't need IN-PACKAGE), call it. The file-based version would let ASDF orchestrate this from cool.asd.
DEFSYSTEM registers the system; FIND-SYSTEM looks it up by name.
ASDF:LOAD-ASD is the function for loading a single .asd file by absolute path. The file-API workflow uses it.

sections.prompt: Define a package GREETER (using CL, exporting GREET) and a function GREETER:GREET (NAME) returning the string "hi, NAME" (lower-case 'hi'). Then define LOAD-GREETER-INLINE () that returns (greeter:greet "agent"). The grader calls LOAD-GREETER-INLINE.
sections.hint: (format nil "hi, ~a" name) inside greeter:greet; (greeter:greet "agent") inside load-greeter-inline.
